entry.title: VFRScenery Releases Walkaround V2 for X-Plane 11/12
entry.summary: Sim: X-Plane. VFRScenery has recently released Walkaround V2 for X-Plane 11 and 12, now accompanied by a follow-up update, v2.3.2. This new version is a full rebuild of the legacy product, now written entirely in native C++, resulting in improved performance, better stability, and smoother transitions throughout. The core concept remains, letting users move freely around their aircraft, but V2 expands this significantly, most notably with a “WalkCabin” mode, allowing the user to transition naturally from exterior to interior. Now included is dynamic audio, including footsteps, environment-based sounds, and pilot/co-pilot dialogue tied to different flight phases. There’s also an integrated landing rate system that displays touchdown data such as vertical speed and G-force, with appropriate commentary. Shortly after release, VFRScenery rolled out v2.3.2, improving stair handling and ground-to-cabin transitions, with added support for double-deck aircraft. Additional fixes address camera positioning issues, while a new door height recording tool aims to improve aircraft compatibility going forward. Walkaround V2 is available now for XP11 and XP12, priced at $19.90 during the introductory period, and is free for existing Version 1 users. You can purchase it through the X-Plane.org Store. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: MK Studios Previews New Porto Airport for MSFS 2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. MK Studios has shared the first preview images of its upcoming Porto Airport (LPPR) for MSFS 2024, revealing a complete rebuild of the scenery, and offering an early look at what is shaping up to be a significantly improved version. The developer said the project has now reached a stage where it feels ready to be shown, giving the community a glimpse at this new version of Porto, which is being built entirely from scratch. A highlight this time around is the use of high-resolution elevation data, allowing MK Studios to properly capture Porto’s distinctive terrain profile, something that has historically been difficult to replicate accurately. The new Porto Airport for MSFS 2024 is currently scheduled for release in April, with further previews and details expected in the lead-up to launch. View the full update post here. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: New Video Showcases EFB for iFly 737 MAX 8 SP1
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. A new preview video from YouTube channel Wheels Up Live has offered us a closer look at the upcoming EFB overhaul for the iFly 737 MAX 8, as iFly lifts its preview embargo. The video was published by an iFly Discord moderator and beta tester, who makes clear throughout that everything shown remains work in progress. The preview focuses on the new tablet-style EFB, which replaces the older layout with a cleaner, more direct interface. Among the features showcased are panel state saving and loading, revised settings pages, manual links, cabin announcements, updated ground services and door controls, SimBrief-linked payload and balance, Navigraph integration, performance tools, failure reporting, display colour customisation, and a new 4x acceleration option. The presenter also shows support for using the EFB on an external iPad. Alongside the video, iFly has confirmed that beta testers are now working with the second SP1 beta, and are allowed to share videos, screenshots, and other previews. There is still no official ETA for release, and the features shown in the current beta may still change before launch. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Blackbird Simulations Releases C-130J Version 1.0.0 for MSFS 2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Blackbird Simulations has released version 1.0.0 of its C-130J for MSFS 2024, touching nearly every system onboard. The update brings a quite substantial list of fixes and refinements, including updates to the flight model, avionics logic, and overall aircraft behaviour, alongside improvements to performance and stability across the board. TCAS has been improved, as well as synchronised animations and effects, including doors, lighting, and various external elements. Sound has also been refined, with tweaks to engine loops, startup sequences, APU behaviour, and environmental effects. On the systems side, Blackbird has addressed a number of navigation and avionics issues, including fixes to airway handling, runway detection, and multiple bugs within the CNI-SP and navigation logic. Fuel system behaviour has been further refined, with more realistic transfer logic, updated pressure calculations, and improved handling of crossfeed and tank limits. The update also introduces various usability improvements, including adjustments to controls, tablet functionality, and interface clarity, while also addressing numerous smaller issues across panels, displays, among others. According to the developer, this release candidate represents a near-final version of the aircraft, with the full public release expected to follow shortly. Read the full changelog here. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Aerosoft Releases Stuttgart Airport Trailer for MSFS 2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Aerosoft has released a trailer for its upcoming Stuttgart Airport (EDDS) for MSFS 2024, headed by the experienced scenery developers Jo Erlend Sund and Lukas Vezyroglou. The new rendition of Stuttgart aims to deliver a highly detailed and immersive experience, built exclusively for MSFS 2024, and taking full advantage of the platform’s latest SDK features. According to Aerosoft, the airport features an up-to-date layout as of March 2026, accurate modelling of buildings and infrastructure, and a custom terrain mesh that captures the airport’s distinct elevation profile. High-resolution textures, realistic night lighting, and custom taxiway signage and lighting are also included. Beyond the core airport, the scenery also includes several nearby landmarks, such as the Stuttgarter Fernsehturm, as well as the Altbach/Deizisau power plant, both featuring custom modelling and lighting effects. The airport environment includes animated interiors, walkable terminal areas in avatar mode, interactive elements, such as elevators and automatic doors, and custom vehicle traffic across the apron. Additional features include custom jetways, GSX integration with dedicated profiles, VDGS support, and AI traffic compatibility with airline-specific parking codes. Aerosoft has not yet confirmed a release date, but notes that Stuttgart Airport is “coming soon” to MSFS 2024. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Threshold at FlightSimWeekend 2026: What to Expect
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. FlightSimWeekend 2026 is almost here, and with it comes one of the biggest gatherings in flight simulation, bringing together developers, creators, hardware manufacturers, and thousands of enthusiasts under one roof in Lelystad, the Netherlands. Taking place on March 21–22 at the Aviodrome Aviation Museum, FlightSimWeekend has grown into the largest dedicated flight simulation event in the world, featuring over 60 exhibitors, close to 5,000 visitors, and a packed schedule of announcements, presentations, and hands-on experiences. Attendees can explore the latest software, hardware, and accessories, try out cutting-edge simulators, and meet the people behind the products shaping the hobby. The event officially kicks off on Friday, March 20, with the Captain’s Dinner, where the Flight Sim Awards 2026 will be presented. Following the success of last year’s inaugural ceremony, the awards return with a more refined, community-driven structure, recognising everything from the best freeware tools and aircraft to hardware, innovation, and long-term contributions to the hobby. Over the weekend, we will also see panels from Microsoft, Navigraph, Aerosoft, FlyByWire Simulations, and many more. Alongside these, attendees can expect product showcases, livestreamed panels, giveaways, and a steady stream of new reveals. We are also expecting major announcements throughout the weekend, including updates from BlueBird Simulations, as well as further details from newer developers such as Vector, who are set to showcase more of their highly anticipated Project Griffin Boeing 787. Beyond the exhibition halls, visitors will also have full access to the Aviodrome Aviation Museum, offering a chance to explore historic aircraft and aviation artefacts. ‍ The weekend is packed with activities, starting Friday evening and continuing across both exhibition days. ‍ See the full schedule here. ‍ If you have not secured your place yet, there is still time to attend: The Captain’s Dinner, which includes the Flight Sim Awards ceremony, is now sold out, but tickets for the main event can still be purchased, using this link. ‍ Take a look at last year's event here: ‍ Threshold will once again be on the ground in Lelystad, attending the event with our largest team to date. Representing Threshold this year are CEO Magnus Lorvik, who is also part of the organising team behind FlightSimWeekend 2026, alongside Sem, Anders, and me, Olai. Throughout the weekend, our team will be active across the show floor, speaking with developers, creators, and exhibitors, while capturing the latest news as it unfolds. As with previous events, we will be bringing you exclusive interviews, behind-the-scenes insights, and hands-on impressions directly from the exhibition floor. Expect a steady flow of content across our social media channels, including Instagram and Facebook, where we will share real-time updates, breaking announcements, and quick looks at new products. For those who prefer a more structured overview, we will also be publishing daily roundup articles, summarising the biggest news, announcements, and highlights from each day of the event. If you are attending in person, keep an eye out for the team, we will be wearing Threshold-branded clothing throughout the weekend, so feel free to come over and say hello! We are looking forward to being there, covering it all, and sharing the experience with you. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Contrail Releases FA50 Trailer
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Contrail has released a brand-new cinematic trailer for its upcoming FA50, offering another look at the developer’s first aircraft project. Originally announced at FSExpo last year, the FA50 marks a significant milestone for Contrail, representing the developer’s first entry into aircraft development, after previously establishing itself as a marketplace platform. Alongside the trailer release, Contrail has also confirmed its presence at FlightSimWeekend 2026, taking place March 21–22 in Lelystad, the Netherlands. Visitors attending the event will be able to try the FA50 firsthand at booth 52, offering a hands-on opportunity to experience the aircraft in action. With FSWeekend set to feature a wide range of announcements, previews, and product showcases, the FA50 is shaping up to be one of the more notable aircraft on display during the event. As always, we will continue to follow developments closely and bring further updates, as it happens, from FlightSimWeekend 2026. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Flight Panels Announces X-Plane Support With First Dedicated Profiles
entry.summary: Sim: X-Plane. Flight Panels is heading beyond Microsoft Flight Simulator for the first time, with the company confirming a new push into X-Plane, after three years spent building Stream Deck profiles for Microsoft’s platform. The Northern Ireland-based developer says the move is its biggest step so far, and one that brings its cockpit-control concept to a completely new group of simmers. For those unfamiliar, Flight Panels builds Stream Deck layouts that turn Elgato hardware into cockpit interfaces, letting the user operate aircraft systems through buttons and touch inputs, instead of relying on a mouse. The company says that approach has already been applied to more than 80 Microsoft Flight Simulator products, and is now being adapted for X-Plane users looking for the same kind of interaction. The first X-Plane releases are planned before the end of March 2026, and will centre on the default Cessna 172 and the Zibo Boeing 737. Flight Panels says both aircraft and autopilot products are in the works, with 15-key and 32-key aircraft layouts planned for the Stream Deck XL, MK.2, Stream Deck Mobile, and Corsair Xeneon Edge, while autopilot variants are set for the Stream Deck +. Distribution is planned through the Flight Panels Store, as well as some third-party marketplaces. For more information on the topic, check out Flight Panels' Newsletter. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Flight Sim Awards Proudly Announces Winners of the 2026 Edition
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. This year’s Flight Sim Awards took place during the Captain’s Dinner at Lelystad, setting the stage for FSWeekend 2026 (set to start tomorrow), celebrating the best products the community had to offer across 22 categories. It was hosted by KatiePilot and NickFlightX, with an engaging pre-show by TwoToneMurphy to introduce the categories. Below is the list of winners, runners-up, and nominees for each of the 22 categories: ‍ ‍ The organization has effusively thanked the community for the support and help with the votes, ensuring vox populi, vox dei would ultimately define the deserving winners of each category. They look forward to continuing to improve it as much as possible, with plans to eventually transform it into a cornerstone event for the community that recognizes and inspires excellence. They also encourage developers, publishers, and creators to continue pushing boundaries and raising the bar for everyone. ‍ The Flight Sim Awards is a collaborative effort between FSWeekend, cruiselevel, Threshold, and FSElite. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: COWS Updates DA40 and DA42 Series for MSFS
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. A new update has been released for the COWS DA40 and DA42 Series, bringing a fresh round of technical refinements to both aircraft, just in time for FSWeekend. One notable addition is full FSCopilot support across the range, as well as various technical improvements to the powerplant and systems. The update is available now through Orbx Direct and the Microsoft Flight Simulator Marketplace. If you are in Lelystad this weekend, COWS will be represented at Flight Sim Weekend 2026 by Tommy, who will be on hand throughout the event. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: iniBuilds Publishes Massive New Aircraft Development Update
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. In a post on their forums, iniBuilds have shared a big update on the development progress of each of their aircraft projects, including the A220, A380, and TriStar for Microsoft Flight Simulator. ‍ The iniBuilds team expands on areas covered in their initial “Welcome to the TriStar” video published last week. One of the big aspects of the TriStar is the three-person cockpit, and iniBuilds have fully simulated this experience through the addition of a Virtual Flight Engineer. Users will be able to activate different flight engineer flows depending on the state of flight, with the flight engineer making relevant callouts and switch sounds playing in the background. Additionally, the iniBuilds TriStar will have automated cabin crew reacting to phases of flight, in what they claim will replicate the “feeling of a fully-operating airliner”. Another major feature on the TriStar is the “Paper Flight Bag” (PFB), a period-correct take on modern Electronic Flight Bags. The PFB will feature everything that users have come to expect in iniBuilds’ previous EFBs - such as SimBrief integration, performance calculations, ground services, customisation options, and loadsheet - as well as controls for the Virtual Flight Engineer, all made to look like real sheets of paper. For the TriStar’s soundset, iniBuilds have partnered with Echo19, and were able to record a real L-1011 airframe to recreate the sounds of the cockpit, cabin, and Rolls-Royce RB211 engines. On the visual side, iniBuilds have spent quite a lot of time working on the distinctive appearance of the TriStar, with much emphasis put on the cabin details and lighting to replicate the ‘golden-era’ aesthetics of airliners at the time, including an optional lounge on the lower deck. Also included is a “POD” variant used to carry a spare fifth engine below the right wing. This will affect the performance of the aircraft with changes being made to procedures and limitations, as well as being reflected in the flight model. The iniBuilds TriStar is set to be released this month, but with no exact release day or pricing being announced yet. At launch, it will be available on the Microsoft Flight Simulator Marketplace, with an iniManager release coming later. FSWeekend 2026 attendees will be able to test it out at iniBuilds’ booth this weekend in Lelystad, Netherlands. ‍ Synaptic Simulation’s upcoming rendition of the Airbus A220 has been progressing well, and approaching release-candidate status. Since their last development showcase at FSExpo 2025, much work has been made by the Synaptic team to overhaul the aircraft’s systems. The A220 now includes a completely reworked fly-by-wire system, a custom engine model, lateral and vertical flight guidance, autopilot, autoland, and fully featured electrical, bleed air, hydraulics, and radio systems. Most notably, the entire flight management system has been rewritten from scratch, which has allowed them to model more advanced interactions between systems, such as automatic navigation and engine model predictions, as well as improved performance. This includes their custom Crew Alerting System - with over 3,000 inputs and accurate timing delay - all using the same logic used in the real aircraft. The Synaptic and iniBuilds teams say that they are about to enter the final stages of testing at the end of the month. ‍ Finally, the iniBuilds team discussed their next big project: the highly anticipated Airbus A380 for Microsoft Flight Simulator. The A380 has been in development for months, and iniBuilds says that they are using the lessons learned from their previous large airliner releases, such as the A340/A350 and Tristar, to guide their development processes. Work on systems and sounds are underway, laying the foundation for the aircraft’s core functionality and overall flight experience. The team have focused on getting high-quality reference materials for every aspect of the aircraft, from systems to visuals and sounds. They have made multiple trips to capture reference photography, measurements, and 3D scans from real aircraft and full-motion simulators to help faithfully recreate the look of the A380. ‍ To celebrate the upcoming TriStar release and FSWeekend, they are running a sale on a range of their long-haul aircraft for Microsoft Flight Simulator iniBuilds have promised they will share more as development of these exciting new aircraft continues, and Threshold will keep you updated every step of the way, so stay tuned! ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: RAVO Flight Systems Reveals First Product, 737NG Overhead Panel FWD
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. A new hardware name has emerged ahead of FSWeekend 2026, with RAVO Flight Systems formally revealing its first product, the 737NG Overhead Panel FWD. This is a full-size forward overhead replica, aimed at home cockpit builders, and training setups. The unit is being sold through Aerosoft, with pre-orders now open, and first deliveries expected from June 2026. According to the product listing, the panel supports Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, X-Plane 11 and 12, and Prepar3D v4 and v5, with plug-and-play connection over USB or Ethernet through RAVO’s bridge software. Launch compatibility includes PMDG’s 737 lines, ProSim738, and the Zibo Mod, while support for the IXEG 737-300, and iFly 737 MAX, is planned after release. They also state that the hardware has been developed around EASA FNPT II and FAA FTD Level 5 design standards, and uses a modular layout that allows individual sections to be expanded, replaced, or removed. The package includes the overhead itself, mounting rails, a 3-metre USB-C to USB-A cable, and power adapters for EU, UK, and USA sockets. Optional electromagnetic switch upgrades, including engine start switches, yaw damper, wing anti-ice, and voice recorder, are planned for summer 2026. If you want to see the panel in person, RAVO says it will be demonstrating the unit at Aerosoft’s booth 33 during FSWeekend in Lelystad, March 21-22. You can pre-order a unit for yourself on the Aerosoft Store for aproximately $3824 USD, excluding taxes. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Microsoft Announces City Update 14, AAU4, and Fokker F27 Friendship
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Microsoft took to the stage at Flight Sim Weekend 2026 in Lelystad this morning with quite an informative keynote, even though Head of Microsoft Flight Simulator Jorg Neumann could not attend in person at the last minute. First up was City Update 14: The Netherlands and Belgium, which is available now, free of charge, for owners of both Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024. The update adds 10 high-fidelity areas of interest built with TIN surface texturing, with eight locations in the Netherlands, including Amsterdam, Rotterdam, Eindhoven, and Utrecht, and two in Belgium, Antwerp and Bruges. Microsoft also confirmed that Aircraft and Avionics Update 4, or AAU4, will arrive with Sim Update 5 in “a few weeks”. This next AAU updates five aircraft to Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024-native standards: the ATR 42-600 and 72-600, the Antonov An-2, the Fokker F.VII, the Latecoere 631, and the Savoia-Marchetti S.55. Microsoft added that more Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 aircraft are expected to be brought forward in future AAU packages as well. Of those, the ATR looks to be one of the biggest parts of the update, with Microsoft highlighting a native Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 version, Career Mode support, navigation and autopilot improvements, TCAS refinements, and a new variant. AAU4 also adds the Avidyne IFD540 and IFD550 as new avionics options for developers, with Working Title presenting them as drop-in alternatives to the older GNS 500 family, and new Cessna 172 Classic variants fitted with those units. Finally, Microsoft closed with Local Legend 23, the Fokker F27 Friendship, developed by iniBuilds. The Dutch-built regional turboprop is available now in the in-sim marketplace for both Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 at $19.99 USD. If you are on the show floor in Lelystad this weekend, Microsoft says AAU4 content is also playable at booth 1. See the floorplan for details. For more information, see the Microsoft Flight Simulator home page. ‍ The Threshold team will continue to cover the news from FSWeekend 2026, live as they break from the show floor. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: WinCtrl Announces New Products
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. WinCtrl has just wrapped up a seismic presentation at FSWeekend 2026 in Lelystad, showcasing a flurry of new products. ‍ WinCtrl impressed the audience with the announcement of the OVHD32 32 SOP, a fully functional overhead panel for the A320. Although specific details about the panel were limited, WinCtrl stated that it will include true-to-scale switches, knobs, and indicators, along with highly realistic control feedback. The OVHD32 SOP is priced at $395.95. WinCtrl has announced the release of new joystick bases. The belt-driven EB Edition will be available in two versions: 23 Nm and 64 Nm. Additionally, the Direct Drive ED version will deliver 16 Nm of torque. The pricing for the belt-driven versions is set at $414.95 for the 23 Nm version and $699.95 for the 64 Nm version. The Direct Drive ED edition will also be available for $699.95. All of the bases are set to have an impressively small footprint. WinCtrl also showcased their Cyber Taurus Rudder Pedals, which now deliver 195 Nm of torque, up from the previous 140 Nm. An even more powerful version was also introduced, offering 590 Nm of torque. The 195 Nm model, named the EB195, is priced at $499.95, while the more powerful EB590, with 590 Nm of torque, will retail at $799.95. WinCtrl continued their presentation by showcasing the Cyber Taurus Yoke, a 1:1 scale replica of a 737 control wheel. According to WinCtrl, the yoke is designed using real data from full flight simulators used in pilot training. They also emphasised that the yoke will provide an “authentic control feel” with 35 cm of travel. A new yoke in the $400-500 segment was also teased, with more details coming soon. WinCtrl has been selling Airbus sidesticks for a long time, but until now, they have never offered a metal version. The new 3XO Metal Fly-By-Wire Joystick Grip is completely made of metal, as the name suggests, and will feature dynamic vibrations. This grip will be compatible with the Orion2 and the new Cyber Taurus Force Feedback bases. It will retail for $69.95 later this year. WinCtrl has not overlooked military aviation enthusiasts. The new Ursa Minor Fighter HOTAS will include a metal core mechanism, realistic trim, and RGB lighting. The base version will retail for $154.95, while the price for the full version has yet to be announced. WinCtrl has announced the next-generation ViperAce throttle grip, designed to provide an authentic aircraft control feel, enhanced dial realism, and a vibration module. The ViperAce Throttle Grip 2 will be available starting in April, priced at $244.06. WinCtrl were keen to show of their next generation MIP UFC Panel 2. The main updates include improved materials, enhanced durability, and a better feel. For example, silicone buttons have been replaced with hard keys. WinCtrl claims that this product will have a longer lifespan, even stating that it is "wear-resistant and aging-proof." It will be available staring in May from $195.6. SimApp Pro is getting a significant update. SimApp Pro 2 will have a completely new user interface. WinCtrl states that the software has been entirely rebuilt to provide a new optimized experience. Aircraft developers have also been brought in as partners. Keep an eye on Threshold for the latest updates from FSWeekend and flight simulation! ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: BlueBird Simulations Reveals 757 Trailer
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. BlueBird Simulations used FSWeekend 2026 in Lelystad to share a trailer for its long-awaited Boeing 757-200, while also reaffirming that this aircraft remains on track for release in Q3 2026. According to the developer, the model shown in the video was already around two weeks old by the time the trailer was presented, suggesting the project has continued to move forward even beyond what we saw on stage. Alongside the new video, BlueBird highlighted several features now in place, including dynamic virtual cockpit night lighting, more than 200 working circuit breakers, custom-coded autothrottle and flight controls, and a functional P61 panel. The developer also said Stage 2 of beta testing is set to begin in the first week of April, pushing toward release later this year. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Moza Announces New Flight Sim Hardware
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. MOZA returns to FSWeekend 2026 with a slew of new hardware for their flight simulation ecosystem. At this year’s event, MOZA showcased several new cockpit products spanning multiple different niches, including the MA3F Electronic Flight Control Module, MA3F Flight Computer Display, MGX1000 Instrument Panel, and FMP18 Panel System. ‍ The MA3F Electronic Flight Control Module (EFCM) focuses on advanced autopilot controls. Inspired by the automation control interfaces used in modern aircraft flight decks, the MA3F allows pilots to manage key flight parameters such as speed, altitude, and navigation modes through tactile rotary encoders and digital display readouts. The MA3F also introduces an extended functionality mode, allowing additional aircraft systems to be mapped directly to the panel. ‍ Working alongside the EFCM, the MA3F Flight Computer Display (FCD) provides a dedicated hardware interface for flight management and navigation input. The device features a high-resolution display, illuminated keypad, and metal front panel designed to replicate real cockpit avionics. Users can use the interface for route entry, navigation management, aircraft configuration, and operational planning. Built-in checklist pages and note panels add additional functionality for complex flight operations. ‍ The MGX1000 Instrument Panel brings a modern glass cockpit avionics experience to flight simulation. The MGX1000 features a 10.4-inch IPS display, tactile rotary encoders, and a layout designed to mirror modern digital instrument panels. Multiple MGX1000 panels can also be combined to recreate complete primary and multifunction instrument displays. ‍ The FMP18 Panel System expands the MOZA ecosystem into combat flight simulation with a modular cockpit control interface inspired by modern combat aircraft. The system combines multi-function display frames, central control panels, and illuminated switches into a configurable cockpit interface. Backlit buttons, rotary encoders, and toggle switches provide clear tactile confirmation, allowing pilots to operate aircraft systems during challenging combat scenarios. ‍ All the new hardware is controlled by MOZA’s in-house Cockpit software, just like their existing products. The software enables pilots to customize input mappings, adjust control parameters, manage lighting effects, and synchronize telemetry across connected devices. ‍ No details on price point or an estimated release date have been announced yet, but Threshold will keep you updated when they do, so stay tuned! If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!
